title: Hardly Kirk Ing

jokes.text: Finally, a kid's show that isn't trying to sell you something. Which reminds me, we need to order more rectangles.
jokes.setup: Homer praising the DVD for not being commercial
jokes.punchline: Immediately followed by his need to 'order more rectangles' from it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - contradicts himself immediately and 'rectangles' is wonderfully absurd

jokes.text: Well, the obvious question is, why did I turn out so academically superior while Bart... While Bart turned out so wonderful in his own way.
jokes.setup: Lisa begins to boast about her academic superiority over Bart
jokes.punchline: Marge's diplomatic interruption to prevent Lisa from insulting Bart
jokes.explanation: Classic family dynamic - Lisa's intellectual vanity checked by Marge's maternal diplomacy

jokes.text: That's the way people talk about Ralph. Ralph, whose favorite color is peanut butter.
jokes.setup: Lisa recognizing the euphemistic way Marge described Bart
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to how people talk about Ralph, with the absurd detail about his favorite color
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to Ralph's character with the perfectly absurd 'peanut butter' detail - economical and memorable

jokes.text: This has been our way of life since we were pitched the idea by reality show producers.
jokes.setup: What seems like a documentary about traditional lifestyle
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's actually a manufactured reality show concept
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-commentary on reality TV's artificial nature, though not deeply character-specific

jokes.text: Honey, this sounds like a noble experiment, but like Prohibition, it will end in a hail of bullets.
jokes.setup: Marge's plan to limit TV watching
jokes.punchline: Homer's dramatic comparison to Prohibition ending violently
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer over-dramatization with historical reference - good structure and character fit

jokes.text: The way children did from 1910 to 2002.
jokes.setup: Marge talking about learning the old-fashioned way from books
jokes.punchline: The oddly specific and recent end date of 2002
jokes.explanation: Subtle humor in the specificity of 2002 as when traditional learning supposedly ended

jokes.text: If I buy you, why do I need all these books?
jokes.setup: Homer looking at an electronic reading device
jokes.punchline: His confusion about why books would still be necessary
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment - misunderstanding technology in character-appropriate way

jokes.text: Who's Milli Vanilli? I don't know, kid. I didn't write this.
jokes.setup: Child asks about Milli Vanilli reference in celebrity-written book
jokes.punchline: Comic Book Guy's dismissive response about not writing the book
jokes.explanation: Meta-joke about celebrity books and Comic Book Guy's characteristic dismissiveness

jokes.text: Sorry, folks, she doesn't even work here.
jokes.setup: Woman doing elaborate story time performance
jokes.punchline: Revelation that she's not actually employed by the store
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - sets up expectation of professional story time then subverts it

jokes.text: Boy, you're pretty pushy for a book I just met.
jokes.setup: Hidden object book giving Homer instructions
jokes.punchline: Homer treating the book like an overly familiar person
jokes.explanation: Clever personification joke that fits Homer's childlike perspective perfectly

jokes.text: Candy cane in the umbrella stand: oldest trick in the book.
jokes.setup: Homer playing hidden object game
jokes.punchline: His confident declaration about hidden object 'tricks' as if he's an expert
jokes.explanation: Homer's false expertise is always funny - treating children's puzzle as serious strategy

jokes.text: If they're not a trumpet or a rolling pin, then no.
jokes.setup: Marge asks if Homer has seen Lisa and Maggie
jokes.punchline: Homer is so focused on hidden objects he can only see those items
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the hidden object game - Homer's tunnel vision taken to absurd extreme

jokes.text: I couldn't get through a day without Doctors Oz, Phil and Gupta.
jokes.setup: Discussing going without TV
jokes.punchline: Marge's specific dependency on TV doctors
jokes.explanation: Mild satire of daytime TV medical shows and Marge's viewing habits

jokes.text: What the hell is an epoxy fight?
jokes.setup: Marge declares 'Epoxy fight!' enthusiastically
jokes.punchline: Her immediate confusion about what she just said
jokes.explanation: Perfect absurdist moment - Marge's enthusiasm followed by immediate bewilderment

jokes.text: Don't worry, I'll clean it up. Get me some whip cream and a safety scissors.
jokes.setup: Marge offering to clean up the epoxy mess
jokes.punchline: Her bizarre choice of cleaning supplies
jokes.explanation: Marge's domestic instincts gone completely wrong - whip cream and safety scissors for epoxy

jokes.text: I always wanted to wear a necktie. Mom says they make you more... ...of a man.
jokes.setup: Milhouse getting dressed up as an adult
jokes.punchline: His innocent and awkward understanding of masculinity
jokes.explanation: Sweet and awkward Milhouse moment - childhood misconceptions about adulthood

jokes.text: Like we go to a movie theater and pay full price?
jokes.setup: Bart explaining the benefits of looking like an adult
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's excitement about paying MORE money
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse - missing the point entirely and being excited about disadvantages

jokes.text: I also want to say that Bart is a remarkable boy, and I disapprove of your fathering and of your fat stomach.
jokes.setup: Milhouse successfully impersonating his father
jokes.punchline: Going too far with increasingly personal insults
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - starts with parenting critique, escalates to personal attack on Homer's appearance

jokes.text: Sometimes it's just so nice to have a man take charge.
jokes.setup: Homer being berated by fake Kirk
jokes.punchline: Homer's pathetic appreciation for being dominated
jokes.explanation: Homer's emasculation played for comedy - his submissive response to authority

jokes.text: I can reach the poisons now, each with an inviting skull and crossbones.
jokes.setup: Milhouse excited about being taller
jokes.punchline: His disturbingly cheerful observation about accessing dangerous chemicals
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny - Milhouse's innocent excitement about dangerous things, 'inviting' skull and crossbones

jokes.text: You know what's itchy and what's not.
jokes.setup: Luann complimenting Kirk on wearing the shirt she bought
jokes.punchline: Kirk's oddly specific and intimate reason for appreciating her clothing choices
jokes.explanation: Perfectly pathetic Kirk moment - finding intimacy in fabric sensitivity knowledge

jokes.text: I wish my dad could have been here to see my parents kiss.
jokes.setup: Milhouse watching his parents reconcile
jokes.punchline: His confused perspective about himself and his father being different people
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about the disguise plot - Milhouse momentarily forgetting he IS pretending to be his dad

jokes.text: I wish my dad could have been here to see my parents kiss.
jokes.setup: Milhouse watching his parents' rare affection
jokes.punchline: Confusing reference to his absent father while father is present
jokes.explanation: Mild confusion joke about Milhouse not recognizing his disguised father, but lacks strong impact.

jokes.text: I rented a truck. I'm driving a truck. I crashed a truck. I rented another truck.
jokes.setup: Milhouse explaining his transportation situation
jokes.punchline: The rapid escalation from rental to crash to immediate re-rental
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation structure - each beat builds logically but absurdly to the next

jokes.text: I'd give you all my money, but my wallet is Velcroed too tight.
jokes.setup: Bullies threatening Milhouse
jokes.punchline: His pathetically specific excuse involving Velcro wallet
jokes.explanation: Perfectly pathetic adult excuse - the Velcro detail makes it wonderfully specific and sad

jokes.text: You're the wussiest adult I've ever seen. An adult-- he's buying it.
jokes.setup: Bully insulting what he thinks is an adult
jokes.punchline: Bart's pleased reaction to being seen as adult despite insult
jokes.explanation: Good irony as Bart takes pleasure in being insulted because it confirms his disguise is working.

jokes.text: Hello, Mr. Kirk. I want to buy this beer, uh, cigarettes, magazines with boobs and three pairs of sunglasses. Polarized.
jokes.setup: Milhouse attempting to buy adult items
jokes.punchline: List escalates to absurd final detail about sunglasses
jokes.explanation: Good child's idea of adult shopping list, with the 'polarized' detail being unexpectedly specific and innocent.

jokes.text: It says those are for her pleasure.
jokes.setup: Adult purchasing scenario at convenience store
jokes.punchline: Apu's matter-of-fact observation about adult products
jokes.explanation: Adult humor played for awkwardness rather than cleverness - functional but not exceptional

jokes.text: Hmm, more money for schools. That could translate into more homework.
jokes.setup: Milhouse considering a voting choice
jokes.punchline: Child's perspective on education funding
jokes.explanation: Perfect child logic about school funding - seeing only the negative personal consequences rather than benefits.

jokes.text: I know this is highly illegal, but you're not leaving this booth till you punch yes. Don't bother screaming for help. This is a municipal off-cycle election. There's no one for miles.
jokes.setup: What appears to be a normal voting situation
jokes.punchline: Election official threatening voter with isolation during off-cycle election
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political satire - the specific detail about off-cycle elections creating isolation is perfect

jokes.text: Their trumpeter has the puffiest cheeks.
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to interest her family in jazz
jokes.punchline: Her childlike focus on the physical aspect of trumpet playing
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Lisa's intellectual interests filtered through child's perspective

jokes.text: The U.S. Embassy has warned people not to go to downtown Springfield.
jokes.setup: Discussion about going downtown
jokes.punchline: The idea that their own town requires embassy warnings
jokes.explanation: Good Springfield-is-terrible joke - embassy warning for domestic location is absurd

jokes.text: Nobody leave till every puppet dead.
jokes.setup: What seems like a children's educational puppet show
jokes.punchline: The performer's disturbing commitment to puppet violence
jokes.explanation: Dark humor in children's entertainment context - the broken English makes it more unsettling

jokes.text: Long before the Teutonic slaughters visited upon our people, there was the massacre by Mstislov of Kiev in 1132.
jokes.setup: Innocent children's puppet show
jokes.punchline: Extremely dark historical violence as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection from children's entertainment to historical trauma. The specific historical reference adds authenticity.

jokes.text: Hello, Lisa. Milhouse? Who's Milhouse? I mean, he-he's my son.
jokes.setup: Lisa recognizing Milhouse in disguise
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's terrible attempt to maintain cover
jokes.explanation: Standard disguise failure humor, saved by Milhouse's characteristic panic and bad recovery.

jokes.text: Thanks, but lowering your voice and standing on paint cans doesn't make you man enough to be my dad.
jokes.setup: Milhouse offering to be their 'adult' guardian
jokes.punchline: Lisa's precise identification of his disguise methods and rejection
jokes.explanation: Lisa's intellectual superiority cutting through the charade - specific and economical

jokes.text: Highlights for... D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer asking about Highlights magazine subscription
jokes.punchline: His realization when reading the full title that it's for children
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness followed by his signature catchphrase

jokes.text: Thimble from Monopoly game. Wondered where that was. $25 casino chip. The smallest nesting doll.
jokes.setup: Homer searching through bed for earring
jokes.punchline: Increasingly bizarre objects found in bed
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of random objects, revealing Homer and Marge's chaotic lifestyle. The casino chip is a nice character detail.

jokes.text: Can you find anything you like in this bed? Just my honey pie.
jokes.setup: Homer finding random objects in their bed
jokes.punchline: His romantic response despite the cluttered bed situation
jokes.explanation: Sweet Homer moment - finding romance despite domestic chaos

jokes.text: Well, if I look at your face, I get sick. Young man, apologize to your sister.
jokes.setup: Milhouse complaining about motion sickness
jokes.punchline: Lisa's harsh comeback followed by Milhouse's authority attempt
jokes.explanation: Good sibling dynamic with Milhouse trying to use fake authority after being insulted.

jokes.text: My adulthood!
jokes.setup: Milhouse getting into a fight and losing his disguise
jokes.punchline: His dramatic cry about losing his adult persona
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse melodrama - treating his fake adulthood as a serious loss

jokes.text: My adulthood!
jokes.setup: Homer grabbing and shaking Milhouse
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's dramatic cry about losing his disguise
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic cry. Milhouse treating his fake adulthood like something precious being destroyed. Great economy.

jokes.text: Pretty shapes tell me to kill everybody.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Baby Poindexter's effects on brain development
jokes.punchline: Bart's disturbing claim about the DVD's influence
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark callback to the opening premise - Bart's brain damage manifesting violently

jokes.text: That's all you pay for screwing up a child for life?
jokes.setup: Learning the settlement amount is $19.95
jokes.punchline: Lisa's outrage at the low compensation for psychological damage
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa moment - her moral outrage at corporate settlements being insufficient

jokes.text: But what if we can't afford not to buy it?
jokes.setup: Bart warning Milhouse not to buy the condo
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's confused logic about affordability
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse - falling for sales logic that makes no sense

jokes.text: How many kids will jazz corrupt?
jokes.setup: Wondering if Lisa went to the jazz club
jokes.punchline: Homer's paranoia about jazz as a corrupting influence
jokes.explanation: Great Homer paranoia - treating jazz as dangerous like drugs or violence

jokes.text: Though, some days, I have a girl buddy on field trips.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming to be single
jokes.punchline: Awkward description of his marriage
jokes.explanation: Homer's childlike understanding of relationships, reducing marriage to casual friendship.

jokes.text: Just looking for a casual hookup? Yeah, for my Thomas the Tank Engines.
jokes.setup: Adult woman asking about casual hookup
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's innocent interpretation about toy trains
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - sexual adult term reinterpreted as innocent toy connection

jokes.text: I love how your arms are too short and thin for your legs. Just like a crocodile.
jokes.setup: Woman finding Milhouse attractive as an adult
jokes.punchline: Her bizarre and unflattering comparison to a crocodile
jokes.explanation: Absurd romantic compliment - comparing proportions to a reptile is wonderfully wrong

jokes.text: The top of your head has a 5:00 shadow.
jokes.setup: Lisa recognizing Milhouse despite disguise
jokes.punchline: Her specific observation about his hair growing back
jokes.explanation: Clever visual joke - applying facial hair terminology to scalp hair growth

jokes.text: Son, promise you will never look like me. I sure hope not.
jokes.setup: Father-son bonding moment with Homer's request
jokes.punchline: Bart's immediate and honest agreement
jokes.explanation: Good father-son dynamic - Homer's self-awareness met with Bart's brutal honesty

jokes.text: That's not a heart-warming thing to end on!
jokes.setup: The episode ending on Bart's harsh response
jokes.punchline: Homer's meta-commentary on the episode's conclusion
jokes.explanation: Meta-joke about episode structure and sitcom conventions - Homer aware of narrative expectations

tmdb_overview: Bart and Milhouse explore the perks of adulthood following a shaving experiment.
